title: Enzyme kinetics
body_html:
Some basic principles:
  • catalysts increase the rate of a chemical reaction
  • they are not consumed and do not alter the position of equilibrium between substrates and products
  • enzyme-catalysed reactions display saturation kinetics, i.e. there is not a linear response to increasing levels of substrate
  • Vmax is the maximum rate of the catalysed-reaction
  • Km is the concentration of substrate that leads to half-maximal velocity. Km = Vmax / 2. Enzymes with a low Km have a high affinity for its substrate

Saturation curve for an enzyme showing the relationship between substrate concentration and reaction rate. This demonstrates the Michaelis-Menten model of a single substrate reaction. Note that as the substrate concentration gets higher the enzyme becomes saturated and the rate reaches Vmax

Linear plots of the Michaelis-Menten model are used to make it easier to estimate Vmaxm.

Lineweaver-Burk plot of kinetic data. Note how the y-intercept = 1/Vmax, therefore as the y-intercept increases Vmax decreases


Competitive inhibitor - competing with the substrate for the enzyme's active binding siteNon-competitive inhibitor - bind outsite the enzyme's active binding site Uncompetitive inhibitor (rare) - binds to the enzyme and enhances the binding of substrate
Effect on VmaxNo change (inhibition can be completely overcome by increasing substrate concentration)DecreasedDecreased
Effect on KmIncreased (takes more substrate to reach Vmax / 2No change (enzyme cannot overcome inhibition by increasing substrate concentration)Decreased
2
